content:
# Revelation Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE, pages 285-286 | **Source Image:** REV_011_page_001.jpg, REV_011_page_002.jpg

1. And there was given to me a reed like a rod, saying, "Rise and measure the temple of God and the altar and those worshipping in it.

2. And the court which [is] outside the temple cast out and do not measure it, for it was given to the nations, and the holy city they shall trample forty-two months.

3. And I shall give [authority] to my two witnesses, and they shall prophesy a thousand two hundred sixty days, having been clothed in sackcloth."

4. These are the two olive trees and the two lampstands which stand before the Lord of the earth.

5. And if anyone wishes to harm them, fire proceeds out of their mouth and devours their enemies; and if anyone should wish to harm them, thus must he be killed.

6. These have the authority to shut the heaven, that rain may not fall [during] the days of their prophecy; and they have authority over the waters to turn them into blood, and to strike the earth with every plague as often as they may wish.

7. And when they finish their testimony, the beast that comes up out of the abyss shall make war with them and shall overcome them and shall kill them.

8. And their corpse [shall be] upon the street of the great city, which is called spiritually Sodom and Egypt, where also their Lord was crucified.

9. And [those] from the peoples and tribes and tongues and nations see their corpse three days and a half, and they do not permit their corpses to be placed into a tomb.

10. And those dwelling upon the earth rejoice over them and make merry, and they shall send gifts to one another, because these two prophets tormented those dwelling upon the earth.

11. And after the three days and a half, [the] spirit of life from God entered into them, and they stood upon their feet, and great fear fell upon those beholding them.

12. And they heard a great voice out of the heaven saying to them, "Come up here." And they went up into the heaven in the cloud, and their enemies beheld them.

13. And in that hour there was a great earthquake, and the tenth of the city fell, and there were killed in the earthquake names of men seven thousand, and the rest became afraid and gave glory to the God of the heaven.

14. The second woe has passed; behold, the third woe comes quickly.

15. And the seventh angel sounded [his trumpet]; and there were great voices in the heaven saying, "The kingdom of the world has become [the kingdom] of our Lord and of his Christos, and he shall reign unto the ages of the ages."

16. And the twenty-four elders who sit before God upon their thrones fell upon their faces and worshipped God,

17. saying, "We give thanks to you, Lord God the Almighty, the [One] who is and the [One] who was, because you have taken your great power and have begun to reign.

18. And the nations were wrathful, and your wrath came, and the time of the dead to be judged, and to give the reward to your servants the prophets and to the holy ones and to those fearing your name, the small and the great, and to destroy those destroying the earth."

19. And the temple of God which [is] in the heaven was opened, and the ark of his covenant was seen in his temple; and there were lightnings and voices and thunders and an earthquake and great hail.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "saying" (legōn):** The participle is masculine nominative, yet grammatically it modifies "reed" (kalamos, masculine). Sinaiticus preserves this — the voice comes through the reed itself or from an unidentified speaker.

2. **v.2 — "cast out" (ekbale exōthen):** Aorist imperative — a single decisive act of exclusion. The outer court is abandoned to Gentile occupation. The period of forty-two months (= 1,260 days = 3.5 years) echoes Daniel 7:25, 12:7.

3. **v.4 — "two olive trees and two lampstands":** Direct allusion to Zechariah 4:3, 11-14. Sinaiticus reads duo elaia kai duo lychniai clearly.

4. **v.8 — "their corpse" (to ptōma autōn):** Sinaiticus reads the singular ptōma (corpse) with plural autōn (their) — a jarring grammatical construction. The two witnesses share a single fate, described as one body. V.9 then shifts to the plural ptōmata.

5. **v.8 — "where also their Lord was crucified":** Sinaiticus reads ho kyrios autōn — "their Lord." This identifies the "great city" as Jerusalem despite its spiritual names Sodom and Egypt.

6. **v.13 — "names of men seven thousand":** Sinaiticus reads onomata anthrōpōn chiliades hepta — literally "names of men thousands seven." The unusual phrasing emphasizes that these are known, named individuals, not anonymous casualties.

7. **v.15 — "The kingdom of the world":** Sinaiticus reads hē basileia tou kosmou — singular "kingdom," not plural. The entire world-system becomes the domain of God and his Anointed One in a single transfer of sovereignty.

8. **v.17 — Omission of "who is to come":** Sinaiticus reads ho ōn kai ho ēn — "the One who is and the One who was" — omitting the expected kai ho erchomenos ("and the One who is coming") found in earlier doxologies (1:4, 4:8). The coming has now been accomplished; the future element is fulfilled.

9. **v.19 — "the ark of his covenant":** hē kibōtos tēs diathēkēs autou — the heavenly original of the lost earthly ark. Sinaiticus reads this clearly. The ark has not been seen since the destruction of Solomon's temple; now it is revealed in the heavenly sanctuary.

— EMET
